## **List-Decodable Mean Estimation in Nearly-PCA Time**

## **Ilias Diakonikolas**

Department of Computer Science University of Wisconsin, Madison Madison, WI 53706 `ilias@cs.wisc.edu`

## **Daniel M. Kane**

Department of Computer Science University of California, San Diego La Jolla, CA 92093 `dakane@cs.ucsd.edu`
**Daniel Kongsgaard** Department of Mathematics University of California, San Diego La Jolla, CA 92093 `dkongsga@ucsd.edu`

**Jerry Li** Microsoft Research Redmond, WA 98052 `jerrl@microsoft.com`

**Kevin Tian** _[∗]_ Department of Computer Science Stanford University Stanford, CA 94305 `kjtian@stanford.edu`

## **Abstract**

Robust statistics has traditionally focused on designing estimators tolerant to a minority of contaminated data. _List-decodable learning_ [CSV17] studies the more challenging regime where only a minority _k_[1][fraction][of][the][dataset,] _[k][≥]_[2][,][is] drawn from the distribution of interest, and no assumptions are made on the remaining data. We study the fundamental task of list-decodable mean estimation in high dimensions. Our main result is a new algorithm for bounded covariance distributions with optimal sample complexity and near-optimal error guarantee, running in _nearly-PCA time_ . Assuming the ground truth distribution on R _[d]_ has identity-bounded covariance, our algorithm outputs _O_ ( _k_ ) candidate means, one of which is within distance _O_ ( _[√] k_ log _k_ ) from the truth.

Our algorithm runs in time _O_[�] ( _ndk_ )[2] , where _n_ is the dataset size. This runtime nearly matches the cost of performing _k_ -PCA on the data, a natural bottleneck of known algorithms for (very) special cases of our problem, such as clustering well-separated mixtures.� � Prior to our work, the fastest runtimes were _O_ ( _n_[2] _dk_[2] ) [DKK20], and _O_ ( _ndk[C]_ ) [CMY20] for an unspecified constant _C ≥_ 6. Our approach builds on a novel soft downweighting method we term SIFT, arguably the simplest known polynomial-time mean estimator in the list-decodable setting. To develop our fast algorithms, we boost the computational cost of SIFT via a careful “win-win-win” analysis of an approximate Ky Fan matrix multiplicative weights procedure we develop, which may be of independent interest.

> _∗_ Part of this work was done as an intern at Microsoft Research.

> 2 � Throughout this work, the _O_ notation hides logarithmic factors in _n_ and the failure probability.

35th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2021).

## **1 Introduction**

Mean estimation has emerged as one of the cornerstone tasks in robust statistics, as the most basic in a hierarchy of increasingly complex estimation problems. The problem is straightforward to state: given samples from a “nice” ground-truth distribution _D_ , where an adversary has (arbitrarily) corrupted a fraction of the data, recover the mean of _D_ as accurately as possible. Due to its fundamental nature, robust mean estimation has received extensive study in the statistics, theoretical computer science, and machine learning communities, starting from the 1960s [Ans60, Tuk60, Hub64, Tuk75].

Despite the apparent simplicity of the problem, efficient algorithms achieving near-optimal error rates were not known in high-dimensional settings until recently [LRV16, DKK[+] 19]. These works studied mean recovery in the traditional setting where a majority of the data is “trusted,” i.e. the fraction of corruptions is strictly less than[1] 2[.][For the standard formulation of robust mean estimation, this] assumption is necessary. Indeed, if only an _α ≤_[1] 2[fraction of points can be trusted, the dataset could] consist of _O_ ( _α_[1][)][ well-separated clusters of “good” points.][The mean of each individual cluster is an] equally valid solution to the estimation problem, so asking for a single solution is ill-posed. In many settings of theoretical and practical interest, having a majority of inlier points is too strong of an assumption. To circumvent the issue of well-posedness in the _α ≤_ 2[1][regime, [][CSV17][] proposed a] relaxed learning notion termed _list-decodable learning_ . Rather than restricting to a single hypothesis, the algorithm may output a list of _O_ ( _α_[1][)][ hypotheses, guaranteeing at least one of them is close to the] truth. In the context of robust mean estimation, this amounts to outputting _O_ ( _α_[1][)][ candidate means.]

A natural problem in its own right, list-decodable mean estimation also generalizes a number of other problems. A prototypical example is learning well-separated mixture models, which has received extensive treatment in the literature [Das99, VW04, AM05, DS07, AK05, RV17, HL18, DKS18, KSS18]. In this problem, data is drawn from a uniform mixture[3] of _k_ “nice” distributions _D_ 1 _, . . . , Dk_ with far apart means, and the goal is to recover clusters coming from each component. By running a list-decodable estimation procedure with _α_ = _k_[1][, each cluster is an equally valid “ground truth,” so the] output must contain candidate means close to each true mean. If the candidates are sufficiently close, standard techniques recover the true clustering. List-decodable mean estimation robustly extends this problem to tolerate adversarial noise or non-uniformity, up to constants in the output size.

Moreover, list-decodable mean estimation can be used to model important data science applications such as crowdsourcing (where a majority of respondents could be unreliable or malicious) [SVC16, MV18], or semi-random community detection in stochastic block models [CSV17]. This primitive is particularly useful in the context of semi-verified learning [CSV17, MV18], where a learner can audit a small amount of trusted data. Even if the trusted dataset is too small to directly learn from, in conjunction with a list-decodable learning procedure it can pinpoint a candidate hypothesis consistent with the verified data (indeed, only roughly log _α_[1][vetted points are required).]

The first tractable algorithm for high-dimensional list-decodable mean estimation was given by [CSV17]. Their work considered the setting where _D_ has (unknown) covariance **Σ** _⪯ σ_[2] **I** for some known _σ_ (i.e. a second moment bound). In this setting, [CSV17] gave a sample-optimal, polynomial-time algorithm outputting _O_ ( _α_[1][)][ candidate means, so some candidate is within] _[ ℓ]_[2][ distance] _O_ ( _σ_ ~~�~~ _α[−]_[1] log _α[−]_[1] ) from the mean of _D_ . As later demonstrated in [DKS18], this error is optimal up to logarithmic factors. However, the [CSV17] algorithm heavily relies on black-box semidefinite programming solvers, and thus the runtime is prohibitively large in high-dimensional instances.

The goal of our work is to develop much faster, truly scalable algorithms for list-decodable mean estimation which achieve optimal statistical guarantees. This goal fits broadly into a larger line of work focused on understanding the computational cost of robustness for basic statistical tasks. In some settings, this line has demonstrated strong evidence that robustness comes at an inherent computational cost [DKS17, HL19]. In contrast, recent algorithms have been developed that achieve robustness essentially “for free” in many other settings [CDG19, DHL19, CDGW19, LY20, JLT20].

While list-decodable mean estimation has received a fair amount of attention, there have only been a few results achieving improved runtimes. One line of work proposed an algorithm framework termed _multi-filtering_ [DKS18, DKK20], based on learning multiple candidate “weight functions.” In particular, [� DKK20] uses this approach to design an algorithm achieving near-optimal error in time _O_ ( _n_[2] _dα[−]_[2] ), where _n_ is the overall dataset size. While this dramatically improves over the runtime
> 3Some algorithms extend beyond the uniform setting; we present it this way for simplicity of exposition.

2

in [CSV17], the quadratic dependence on _n_ is not ideal in high-dimensional settings. Concurrently, the work [CMY20] proposes a different, descent-based algorithm based on (approximate) positive semidefinite programming, achieving optimal error (up to constant factors) in time _O_[�] ( _ndα[−][C]_ ) for some constant _C ≥_ 6. When _α_ = Θ(1), the [CMY20] runtime is nearly-linear in the problem input size. However, if _α[−]_[1] scales polynomially with _d_ , e.g. a mixture model with many components in moderate dimension, then this large dependence on _α[−]_[1] may also be prohibitively slow.

In contrast to this somewhat murky runtime landscape, the state of affairs for clustering separated mixture models is relatively clear. The fastest known algorithm for clustering a mixture of _k_ wellseparated components is almost twenty years old [VW04], and runs in time _O_[�] ( _ndk_ ), as a relatively simple and elegant application of (approximate) _k_ -principal components analysis ( _k_ -PCA). Since list-decodable mean estimation can be thought of as the natural robust analog to clustering mixture models, it is natural to ask:

_Can we perform list-decodable mean estimation as efficiently as learning mixture models?_

Concretely, since clustering mixture models is an instance of list-decodable learning with _α_ = _k[−]_[1] , the question becomes: can we solve list-decodable mean estimation in time _O_[�] ( _[nd] α_[)][?][This presents] itself as a natural barrier, since further improvement would imply faster learning of mixture models.

## **2 Our results**

Our main contribution is to answer this question affirmatively with near-optimal statistical guarantees. We start by formally defining the problem we study, which we call “list-decodable mean estimation.” **Definition 1.** _Given T ⊂_ R _[d] and_ 0 _< α ≤_[1] 2 _[such that an][ α][-fraction of][ T][are independent draws] from a distribution D with unknown target mean µ[∗] and unknown covariance_ **Σ** _⪯ σ_[2] **I** _, output a_ ˆ ˆ _small list of vectors {µj}j∈L such that_ min _j∈L ∥µj − µ[∗] ∥_ 2 _is minimized._

We emphasize that no assumptions are made on the remaining 1 _− α_ fraction of _T_ , i.e. they may be arbitrary and chosen by a computationally unbounded adversary allowed to inspect the inliers. In the setting of Definition 1, our main result states that we can solve list-decodable mean estimation nearlyoptimally in the time it takes to perform _k_ -PCA poly-logarithmically many times, for _k_ = Θ( _α_[1][)][.]

**Theorem 1** (See Corollary 3 of the supplementary material) **.** _Given |T |_ = _n_ = Ω( _α[d]_[)] _[ samples, there] is an algorithm with runtime O_[�] � _ndα_ � _that with high probability outputs a list of m vectors {µj}j∈_ [ _m_ ] _, for m_ = _O_ ( _α_[1][)] _[, such that]_[ min] _[j][∈]_[[] _[m]_[]] _[ ∥][µ][∗][−][µ][j][∥]_[2][=] _[ O]_[(] _[σα][−]_ 2[1][�] log _α[−]_[1] ) _._

A few remarks are in order. First, a list size of Ω( _α_[1][)][,][sample][complexity][of][Ω(] _α[d]_[)][,][and][error] of Ω( _σα[−]_[1] 2 ) are information-theoretically necessary for this problem [DKS18]. We also provide an algorithm in the supplementary material returning _m_ = _O_ ( _α_[1][)][ hypotheses] _[ {][µ][j][}][j][∈]_[[] _[m]_[]][attaining] optimal error min _j∈_ [ _m_ ] _∥µ[∗] − µj∥_ 2 = _O_ ( _σα[−]_ 2[1] ), with an additive runtime overhead of _O_[�] ( _α[−]_[6] ). Without loss of generality _α[−]_[1] = _o_ ( _d_ ), as otherwise there is a trivial algorithm (cf. Appendix A of the supplementary material), so the _α[−]_[6] additive term is only dominant in the range _α[−]_[1] _∈_ [ _ω_ ( _√d_ ) _, o_ ( _d_ )] assuming _n_ = Θ( _α[d]_[)][. Even with this overhead, our runtime is the best-known in all parameter regimes.] Our approach is inspired by the way in which fast algorithms for robust mean estimation in the _α →_ 1 regime were built. First, a “simple” polynomial (but not nearly-linear) time algorithm — namely, the filter — was developed [DKK[+] 19, DKK[+] 17, Ste18]. Then, building on the framework of this simple algorithm, near-linear time algorithms were developed [CDG19, DHL19] using tools from the continuous optimization community — specifically, matrix multiplicative weights regret analyses. In this paper, we accomplish both these steps for the _α ≪_[1] 2[regime.][First, we design a simple “basic”] algorithm for the list-decodable mean estimation problem, and then speed it up using matrix regret minimization tools. As explained below, both steps require substantially new ideas.

SIFT **: a new, simple algorithm for list-decodable learning.** Our first main contribution is a novel algorithm for list-decodable mean estimation, which we call SIFT (Subspace Isotropic FilTering),[4]
4After the initial dissemination of this work, we were made aware there is another (unrelated) algorithm named SIFT developed in the computer vision community [Low99].

Table 1: List-decodable mean estimation algorithm runtimes. All algorithms listed return lists of size _O_ ( _α_[1][)][ and use sample complexity] _[ n]_[ =] _[ O]_[(] _α[d]_[)][, which are information-theoretically optimal [][DKS18][].] The _O_[�] notation hides polylogarithmic factors in failure probability and dimension.

achieving optimal statistical guarantees in time _O_[�] ( _[n] α_[2] _[d]_[)][.][While by itself,][ SIFT][ does not achieve a] nearly-linear runtime, its framework is vital in designing our more sophisticated algorithms. Crucially, SIFT is conceptually different from all prior approaches for our problem, with a complete decoupling of a “filtering” step and a “clustering” step; it is these differences that allow for our later speedups. The main advantage of SIFT is its simplicity. All prior algorithms for list-decodable mean estimation were quite complicated, with involved and lengthy analyses, whereas an analysis of SIFT fits in just over two pages. We believe SIFT is of independent interest (both theoretically and practically), and its framework may find applications in other list-decodable learning settings.

FastSIFT **: speeding up** SIFT **via Ky Fan regret minimization.** While each SIFT iteration can be performed in time _O_[�] ( _ndk_ ), the algorithm requires Θ( _n_ ) iterations in the worst case, as there are instances where each iteration removes only one data point. The main challenge is to combine the SIFT analysis with a downweighting procedure guaranteeing termination in _polylogarithmically_ many iterations. To achieve this, we use tools from semidefinite programming (SDP) to design iterative schemes with stronger termination guarantees. This mirrors, and is inspired by, the _α →_ 1 regime, where tools such as packing SDP solvers [CDG19] and matrix multiplicative weights [DHL19] were used to speed up the basic filter. We note that [CMY20] also uses SDP tools to obtain their runtime, but in a substantially different way: they design a black-box Ky Fan norm packing SDP subroutine, whereas we directly use a (stronger) regret guarantee to guide weight removal. Finally, while there have been substantial recent runtime advances for solving SDPs (see e.g. [JKL[+] 20]), these guarantees are too weak for our purposes, requiring the development of new approximate SDP tools.

## **4 Warmup:** SIFT **algorithm and analysis**

We develop a conceptually new and simple algorithm for solving list-decodable mean estimation based on a “soft downweighting” approach, which forms the backbone of our faster methods. In brief, its design philosophy can be summarized as follows: first learn the mean in all but Θ( _α_[1][)][ directions,] and then randomly sample in the remaining subspace. All omitted proofs are either straightforward or from prior work, and are given in Section 3 of the supplementary material. We define two useful concepts in analyzing our downweighting methods.

## **5 Nearly-PCA time algorithm: overview of** FastSIFT

In this section, we highlight the main technical ideas behind our fast algorithms. In particular, we show how to leverage tools built in developing SIFT and combine them with a weight removal scheme based on a Ky Fan variant of the matrix multiplicative weights (MMW) regret minimization framework we develop, to give our final algorithm. Throughout this overview, we define integer _k_ = Θ( _α_[1][)][to][represent][some][dimensionality][of][a][linear][subspace;][constants][will][be][specified][in] relevant algorithms in the supplementary material.

7

**Scoring via Ky Fan matrix multiplicative weights.** To obtain the main result of this paper, it remains to show how we can improve the number of iterations of SIFT to polylogarithmic. For this, we turn to a strategy originating in [DHL19] in the large- _α_ regime, which is to use the _matrix multiplicative weights_ regret minimization framework to define weights for stronger performance guarantees. The intuition is that by using scores defined by more than the top eigenvector of the current covariance matrix (or in this paper, the top _k_ eigenvectors), we capture more than one bad point at a time and obtain better worst-case iteration bounds. The main MMW regret guarantee makes this formal. Roughly, it says that if in each iteration we can downweight the current covariance so that its inner product with a matrix given by the MMW framework is small, then in _O_ (log _d_ ) iterations we can halve the operator norm (the maximal inner product against _all_ trace-1 nonnegative matrices).

A key technical contribution of this paper is to give a Ky Fan _k_ -norm (sum of _k_ largest eigenvalues) generalization of MMW, which typically gives operator norm guarantees. We analyze our algorithm and show that it is tolerant to the error guarantees of approximate _k_ -PCA procedures such as simultaneous power iteration [MM15]. Crucial to our tightest runtime bounds are strengthenings of the analysis of a similar procedure found in [CMY20] in several places, which save multiple _k_ factors in our guarantees and may be of independent interest; we now highlight a few here.[6]

The main idea of our Ky Fan MMW regret guarantee is to bound the cost of actions _{_ **Y** _t}t≥_ 0 against a sequence of positive semidefinite “gain matrices” _{_ **G** _t}t≥_ 0 as measured by inner products. The actions _{_ **Y** _t}t≥_ 0 are given by the algorithm (depending on the gain matrices), and live in

The reason for this choice of action set, the “ _k_ -Fantope,” is because it satisfies sup **U** _∈Y ⟨_ **U** _,_ **G** _⟩_ = _∥_ **G** _∥k_ , so the best action over _Y_ in hindsight captures this norm. Ultimately, our re-weighting scheme requires matrix-vector query access to each **Y** _t_ , which are defined by _Bregman projections_ onto the set _Y_ . coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper presents a new algorithm, SIFT, for list-decodable mean estimation under identity-bounded covariance, achieving a runtime of nearly-PCA time. The authors combine a novel soft-downweighting approach with an approximate Ky Fan matrix multiplicative weights procedure to improve upon the runtime of existing methods.

The paper provides a significant theoretical contribution to robust statistics by matching the runtime of $k$-PCA for list-decodable mean estimation. The 'win-win-win' analysis of the MMW procedure is a technically sophisticated approach that effectively addresses the computational bottleneck of prior work. The results are well-situated within the existing literature and represent a clear advance in algorithmic efficiency.

Overview issues:

- Ambiguity in the 'win-win-win' analysis logic: In Section 5, the authors describe a 'win-win-win' analysis for the MMW procedure, but the conditions under which the algorithm transitions between the three 'win' cases are not explicitly formalized. Specifically, the paper mentions that the algorithm restarts when the $k$-th largest eigenvalue is smaller than half the largest, or when certain inner product criteria are met, but it does not provide a formal state-transition proof. Without a clear description of the control flow and a proof that these conditions cover all possible failure modes, the convergence guarantee remains heuristic. I suggest adding a formal algorithm block for the FastSIFT controller that explicitly defines the branching logic and the termination conditions for each phase.

- Dependence on the $\alpha^{-6}$ additive term: Theorem 1 and the accompanying discussion note an additive runtime overhead of $\widetilde{O}(\alpha^{-6})$ to achieve optimal error. While the authors claim this is only dominant in a specific parameter regime, it is unclear how this term interacts with the $\widetilde{O}(nd\alpha^{-1})$ term in practice for moderate $d$ and $\alpha$. The paper should provide a more detailed analysis of the crossover point where the $\alpha^{-6}$ term becomes the bottleneck. A plot or a more rigorous breakdown of the runtime components would clarify the practical utility of the algorithm for different values of $\alpha$.

- Justification of the approximate Ky Fan MMW update: The paper relies on a new approximate Ky Fan MMW update to achieve the claimed runtime, but the derivation of the divergence bound in Proposition 4 is only sketched. The reliance on the Alexandrov theorem for the Hessian of the spectral function is a powerful tool, but the application here requires careful verification of the twice-differentiability of the conjugate $r^*$ in the presence of approximate power iteration. The authors should provide a more complete derivation in the appendix or a more detailed justification for why the approximation error does not accumulate over the $O(\log d)$ iterations to exceed the required precision.

- Lack of clarity on numerical stability: The algorithm involves repeated applications of simultaneous power iteration and matrix-vector products with $\Sigma^{-1/2}$ (where $\Sigma$ is the empirical covariance). In high dimensions, these operations are susceptible to numerical instability, especially when the covariance matrix is ill-conditioned or the weights become very small. The paper assumes exact arithmetic, which is standard in theory, but it would be beneficial to include a brief discussion or a lemma regarding the stability of the SIFT updates under floating-point perturbations. This is particularly relevant given the iterative nature of the weight removal process.

- Absence of a concrete worked example for the SIFT algorithm: The paper introduces the SIFT method as a novel, conceptually simple algorithm for list-decodable mean estimation, yet it provides no concrete, fully-specified numerical example of its operation. Readers lack a demonstration of how the 'soft downweighting' mechanism behaves on a simple, low-dimensional dataset, such as a mixture of two Gaussians in 2D. Computing the SIFT iterations for a specific, small-scale instance would clarify how the weight vector $w$ evolves and how the 'saturated' property is maintained in practice. This addition would serve as a vital sanity check for the reader to calibrate their intuition before engaging with the more complex FastSIFT procedure.

- Lack of empirical validation for the 'win-win-win' transition logic: The 'win-win-win' analysis is central to the paper's claim of achieving nearly-PCA runtime, yet the paper treats the transition between the three cases (restarting phases, setting aside subspaces, and binary search weight removal) as a theoretical framework rather than a verifiable algorithmic flow. A small-scale simulation showing the frequency of these transitions on a synthetic dataset—where the ground truth is known—would demonstrate that the logic is not just theoretically sound but effectively triggers the expected progress. Without such a demonstration, the claim that the algorithm 'nearly matches' the cost of performing $k$-PCA remains abstract and difficult to verify as a practical implementation guide.

Recommendation: accept

Revision targets:

- Formalize the state-transition logic for the 'win-win-win' analysis in the FastSIFT controller.

- Clarify the runtime dominance of the $\alpha^{-6}$ additive term relative to the $\alpha^{-1}$ term for relevant parameter ranges.

- Expand the derivation of the divergence bound in Proposition 4 to ensure the approximation error accumulation is rigorously bounded.

comments.title: Ambiguity in runtime dominance threshold
comments.quote: the $\alpha^{-6}$ additive term is only dominant in the range $\alpha^{-1}\in[\omega(\sqrt{d}),o(d)]$ as
comments.explanation: The runtime of the main algorithm is given as O~(nd/alpha). With n = Theta(d/alpha), the runtime is O~(d^2/alpha^2). The additive overhead O~(alpha^{-6}) becomes dominant when alpha^{-6} > d^2/alpha^2, which simplifies to alpha^{-4} > d^2, or alpha^{-1} > sqrt{d}. The range [omega(sqrt{d}), o(d)] appears to be an error, as the overhead is dominant for all alpha^{-1} in the range [sqrt{d}, d]. Please re-evaluate this threshold.

comments.title: Ambiguous runtime notation
comments.quote: Our algorithm runs in time $\widetilde{O}(ndk)^2$
comments.explanation: The notation O~(ndk)^2 is ambiguous. If the intended runtime is O~(ndk^2), please write it as such. If the exponent is intended to apply to the entire complexity class, the notation is non-standard. Please clarify the dependence on k to ensure the comparison to k-PCA is clear.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces SIFT and FastSIFT for list-decodable mean estimation, claiming near-optimal statistical guarantees with nearly-PCA runtime. The approach combines a novel soft downweighting scheme with a Ky Fan variant of matrix multiplicative weights. While the theoretical framework is ambitious and the runtime improvement is significant, the main text relies heavily on deferred proofs and informal statements for its core technical contributions.

The paper makes a notable contribution to the computational complexity of list-decodable mean estimation by introducing a simpler filtering algorithm and accelerating it via matrix multiplicative weights. The theoretical framework is sound in outline, and the connection to Ky Fan norms is a natural extension of prior work. However, the presentation defers critical technical details to the supplementary material, which hinders immediate verification of the main claims.

Overview issues:

- Abstract Runtime Typo: The abstract states the algorithm runs in time $\widetilde{O}(ndk)^2$, which directly contradicts the "nearly-PCA time" claim and Theorem 1's $\widetilde{O}(nd/\alpha)$ bound. Since $k = \Theta(1/\alpha)$, the squared notation introduces a quadratic dependence on $n$ that the paper explicitly aims to avoid. This typo undermines confidence in the technical precision of the introduction. The authors should correct this to $\widetilde{O}(ndk)$ or $\widetilde{O}(nd/\alpha)$ and verify all asymptotic notations throughout the manuscript.

- Deferred Core Analysis Hinders Verification: Sections 5 and 6 outline the FastSIFT algorithm and the "win-win-win" analysis, but explicitly defer rigorous proofs to the supplementary material. Key technical propositions, such as the Ky Fan MMW regret guarantee and the approximate PCA bounds, are presented informally or with unspecified constants. For a theory paper, readers need enough detail in the main text to verify the logical flow. The authors should move the precise statements of Propositions 3 and 4 into the main text and provide a complete sketch of the win-win-win case analysis, or at least specify the exact parameters and error tolerances required for each phase.

- Error Propagation in Approximate k-PCA Integration: The FastSIFT algorithm relies on simultaneous power iteration to compute approximate top-$k$ eigenvectors within the MMW update step. Proposition 3 claims an approximation factor of $1 \pm \epsilon$, improving on prior work. However, the main text does not specify how many power iteration steps are required to achieve this $\epsilon$, nor does it detail how approximation errors accumulate across the $O(\log d)$ MMW iterations. Since the runtime claim $\widetilde{O}(nd/\alpha)$ depends on keeping the total overhead low, the authors must clarify the trade-off between approximation accuracy and iteration count. A formal lemma bounding the cumulative error in the MMW regret bound would strengthen the runtime analysis.

- Absence of Empirical Validation: The paper claims significant runtime improvements over prior methods like DKK20 and CMY20, but provides no simulations or empirical comparisons. In computational statistics and learning theory, demonstrating that the theoretical speedups translate to practical gains, especially regarding hidden constants and the behavior of approximate PCA solvers, is standard practice. Adding a brief experimental section with synthetic data would validate the claimed $\widetilde{O}(nd/\alpha)$ scaling and show how the algorithm performs in finite-sample regimes. This would also help readers assess the practical feasibility of the "nearly-PCA" claim.

- Parameter Sensitivity and Practical Feasibility: The algorithm assumes a known or easily estimable covariance bound $\sigma$ and relies on precise tuning of the Ky Fan MMW step size $\eta$. The main text does not discuss how sensitive the performance is to these parameters or how to adapt the method when the bounded covariance assumption is only approximately satisfied. Given that the method uses approximate eigenspace projections, small violations of the covariance bound could destabilize the weight removal process. A discussion on robustness to parameter misspecification or a practical heuristic for tuning $\eta$ and $\epsilon$ would make the contribution more accessible to practitioners.

- Worked example on a concrete mixture model: The paper presents abstract algorithms and regret bounds but never computes them on a fully specified low-dimensional model. Readers will struggle to calibrate the intuition behind the soft downweighting and the win-win-win analysis without seeing how the algorithm behaves on a simple instance. We should trace the SIFT and FastSIFT steps for a concrete model, such as a mixture of two Gaussians in $ℝ^2$ or $ℝ^3$ with a known adversarial corruption pattern. Computing the weight updates, the Ky Fan norm decay, and the final candidate list for this example would demonstrate that the theoretical guarantees are non-vacuous. It would also let readers verify that the geometric mechanism driving the algorithm actually isolates the inliers as claimed.

- Verification of mixture clustering as a special case: The introduction explicitly frames list-decodable mean estimation as a robust generalization of clustering well-separated mixture models. It notes that $k$-PCA solves the latter in $ĸ(ndk)$ time and asks whether the new algorithm can match this efficiency. The paper never formally verifies that its procedure recovers the standard spectral clustering result when the mixture components are well-separated and $α = 1/k$. To deliver on this framing, the authors should add a paragraph showing how the FastSIFT updates align with classical $k$-PCA in this regime. Demonstrating that the Ky Fan MMW procedure collapses to standard power iteration when eigenvalues are well-separated would validate the claim that the algorithm nearly matches the mixture clustering bottleneck.

- Implementation guidance for maintaining covariance updates: The claimed $ĸ(nd/α)$ runtime relies on efficiently updating the weighted covariance matrix and computing approximate $k$-PCA across polylogarithmic MMW iterations. The main text outlines the high-level steps but omits the data structures or incremental update rules required to avoid recomputing the full covariance from scratch at each step. In practice, maintaining the weighted second moment matrix under the soft downweighting scheme requires careful bookkeeping to stay within the stated time bound. A brief discussion of how to implement the weight updates and approximate eigenspace projections using standard matrix sketching or incremental covariance tracking would bridge the gap between the theoretical analysis and a runnable implementation. Without this guidance, practitioners cannot assess whether the theoretical speedups survive hidden constants or numerical overhead.

Recommendation: Major revision. The theoretical contribution is strong and the runtime improvement is meaningful, but the heavy reliance on supplementary material for core proofs and the informal treatment of key propositions prevent a complete assessment of correctness. Additionally, the runtime typo in the abstract and lack of empirical validation need to be addressed.

Revision targets:

- Correct the runtime notation in the abstract and verify all asymptotic bounds throughout the text.

- Move precise statements of Propositions 3 and 4 into the main text and provide a complete, self-contained sketch of the win-win-win analysis.

- Specify the exact iteration complexity for the approximate k-PCA subroutine and prove a formal bound on error accumulation across MMW steps.

- Include a simulation section demonstrating the algorithm's runtime scaling and error performance on synthetic datasets, comparing against baseline methods.

- Add a discussion on parameter sensitivity, particularly regarding the step size $\eta$, approximation tolerance $\epsilon$, and robustness to covariance bound misspecification.

comments.title: Unexplained factor of 2 in Ky Fan MMW regret bound
comments.quote: $\left\|\frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=0}^{T-1}\mathbf{G}_{t}\right\|_{k}\leq\frac{2}{T}\sum_{t=0}^{T-1}\left\langle\mathbf{G}_{t},\mathbf{Y}_{t}\right\rangle+\frac{k\log d}{\eta T}.$ (2)
comments.explanation: The coefficient 2 in front of the average inner product sum appears without justification. Standard matrix multiplicative weights analyses with matrix entropy regularization typically yield a coefficient of 1 on the regret term. The extra factor likely comes from a loose intermediate bound or a typographical error. Please verify the derivation in the supplementary material and either tighten the bound to 1 or explicitly point to the step where the factor of 2 arises.

comments.title: Missing derivation for the 2/5 coefficient in regret bound
comments.quote:
If we can guarantee that every $\left\langle\mathbf{G}_{t},\mathbf{Y}_{t}\right\rangle$ is bounded by, say, $\frac{1}{5}\left\|\mathbf{G}_{0}\right\|_{k}$, and $k\left\|\mathbf{G}_{0}\right\|_{\text{op}}\leq 2\left\|\mathbf{G}_{0}\right\|_{k}$ (i.e. the top $k$ eigenvalues of $\mathbf{G}_{0}$ are roughly uniform), the above regret guarantee becomes

$\left\|\mathbf{G}_{T}\right\|_{k}\leq\left\|\frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=0}^{T-1}\mathbf{G}_{t}\right\|_{k}\leq\frac{2}{5}\left\|\mathbf{G}_{0}\right\|_{k}+\frac{2\left\|\mathbf{G}_{0}\right\|_{k}\log d}{T}\,.$
comments.explanation: The transition to the $\frac{2}{5}$ coefficient skips several algebraic steps. The authors substitute $\eta = \|\mathbf{G}_0\|_{\text{op}}^{-1}$ into the regret bound, but the manipulation required to combine the linear and logarithmic terms into the stated fraction is not shown. Please explicitly write out the substitution and show how the condition $k\|\mathbf{G}_0\|_{\text{op}} \leq 2\|\mathbf{G}_0\|_k$ is used to bound the operator norm terms by the Ky Fan norm. This will make the convergence rate transparent.

comments.title: Misleading claim about random sampling for subspace mean estimation
comments.quote: To learn the mean in $\mathbf{B}$, there are a number of much simpler strategies such as random sampling from the dataset, which we discuss when describing ListDecodableMeanEstimation in the sequel.
comments.explanation: Random sampling from the full dataset cannot reliably estimate the mean in the subspace $\mathbf{B}$ under list-decodable assumptions. Since only a $1/k$ fraction of the data is inliers, a uniform sample will pick a good point with probability $1/k$. A single sample or a small batch does not yield a robust estimate without additional list-decodable machinery or careful weighting. The text later mentions running SIFT on the subspace as an alternative, which suggests standard sampling is insufficient. Clarify why random sampling is viable here, or remove the claim to avoid confusing readers about the algorithm's robustness guarantees.

comments.title: Ambiguity in weight update schedule versus batch analysis
comments.quote: norm. Our strategy, following *[x12]*, is to let $\mathbf{G}_{t}$ be the empirical covariance matrix with respect to $w_{t}$, for monotonically decreasing weight sequence $\{w_{t}\}_{t\geq 0}$ formed by safe weight removals
comments.explanation: The description of the update schedule contains a tension. The text introduces a monotonically decreasing weight sequence but later specifies that weight removals via binary search occur only "Whenever $\Theta(\log d)$ iterations pass". If weights remain constant across a batch of iterations, the covariance matrix $\mathbf{G}_t$ does not decrease during those steps. This breaks the monotonicity assumption required for the fixed step size $\eta = \|\mathbf{G}_0\|_{\text{op}}^{-1}$. Please clarify whether the monotonicity condition applies to the sequence of covariances computed at each step, or only to the sequence after each batch update, and adjust the regret analysis to match the actual update frequency.

comments.title: Non-standard spectral approximation claim in Proposition 3
comments.quote:
n with high probability,

$$
(1 - \epsilon) \mathbf {S} \preceq \mathbf {P S P} + (\mathbf {I} - \mathbf {P}) \mathbf {S} (\mathbf {I} - \mathbf {P}) \preceq (1 + \epsilon) \mathbf {S}.
$
comments.explanation: This inequality asserts that the block-diagonalization of $\mathbf{S}$ in the approximate eigenspace is spectrally close to $\mathbf{S}$ itself. For exact eigenvectors, the middle term equals $\mathbf{S}$ and the bound is trivial. For approximate eigenvectors, standard power iteration guarantees bound the error in the Ky Fan norm or the operator norm of the residual, rather than preserving the entire spectrum up to $(1\pm\epsilon)$. The stated form is misleading because it implies a stronger spectral preservation than simultaneous power iteration typically provides. Replace this with a standard subspace error bound, such as $\|\mathbf{S} - \mathbf{P S P}\|_{\mathrm{op}} \leq \epsilon \lambda_{k+1}(\mathbf{S})$, to align with established PCA analysis.

comments.title: "Boost" contradicts goal of fast algorithms
comments.quote: To develop our fast algorithms, we boost the computational cost of SIFT via a careful "win-win-win" analysis
comments.explanation: The word "boost" literally means to increase, which clashes with the paper's goal of developing faster algorithms. The authors clearly intend to improve efficiency. Change "boost the computational cost" to "reduce the computational cost" or "boost the efficiency" to avoid confusing readers.

comments.title: Inverted failure probability bound direction
comments.quote: Proposition 1 implies that this assumption holds for any failure probability larger than $\exp(-\Omega(d))$ (up to constants in parameter definitions); for any smaller failure probability, Proposition 1 implies that the assumption still holds by adjusting the sample size by a logarithmic factor.
comments.explanation: The phrasing "failure probability larger than $\exp(-\Omega(d))$" is logically inverted. High-probability guarantees require the failure rate to be bounded *above* by the exponential term. Stating it holds for a "larger" failure probability implies a weaker guarantee, which contradicts the subsequent clause about handling "smaller" failure probabilities. Rewrite "larger than" to "smaller than" to match standard probabilistic bounding conventions.

comments.title: Inconsistent claim regarding SIFT's error optimality
comments.quote: achieving optimal sample complexity and error, at a logarithmic overhead in list size and an $O(n)$ overhead in runtime compared to our final method.
comments.explanation: The abstract characterizes the main algorithm's guarantee as "near-optimal error," which implies the baseline SIFT algorithm falls short of optimality. Theorem 2 bounds SIFT's error at $O(1/\alpha)$, while the information-theoretic limit is $O(\sqrt{\frac{\log k}{\alpha}})$. Stating that SIFT achieves "optimal ... error" here misrepresents its theoretical performance relative to the abstract's framing. Adjust the wording to acknowledge that SIFT achieves optimal sample complexity but only near-optimal error.

comments.title: Undefined function f in Proposition 4 Bregman divergence
comments.quote: let  $V_{\mathbf{A}}^{r^*}(\mathbf{B}) := f(\mathbf{B}) - f(\mathbf{A}) - \langle \nabla f(\mathbf{A}), \mathbf{B} - \mathbf{A} \rangle$  be the induced Bregman divergence.
comments.explanation: The symbol $f$ appears in the definition of the Bregman divergence but is never introduced in the surrounding text. Given the notation $V_{\mathbf{A}}^{r^*}$ and the context of convex conjugates, this is a typo for $r^*$. Rewrite the definition as $V_{\mathbf{A}}^{r^*}(\mathbf{B}) := r^*(\mathbf{B}) - r^*(\mathbf{A}) - \langle \nabla r^*(\mathbf{A}), \mathbf{B} - \mathbf{A} \rangle$ to align with standard notation.

comments.title: Non-standard solid simplex definition
comments.quote: We denote the (solid) probability simplex in  $n$  dimensions by  $\Delta^n = \{w \in \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}^n \mid \| w \|_1 \leq 1\}$
comments.explanation: The definition uses $\|w\|_1 \leq 1$, which defines the solid simplex rather than the standard probability simplex where weights sum to exactly 1. While the normalization factor $\|w_{T'}\|_1$ in the mean and covariance formulas correctly handles unnormalized weights, this non-standard definition may conflict with optimization routines or theoretical results that assume $\sum w_i = 1$. Consider changing the definition to $\|w\|_1 = 1$ or explicitly noting that the solid simplex is used to allow unnormalized weights throughout the analysis.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper presents a significant algorithmic improvement for list-decodable mean estimation, achieving nearly-PCA time with near-optimal error guarantees. The SIFT algorithm and its accelerated version via Ky Fan MMW are novel contributions. The theoretical analysis is rigorous and the comparison with prior work is clear. The paper is well-structured and meets the standards of a top venue like NeurIPS.

Overview issues:

- Runtime guarantee validation: The paper claims a runtime of ~O(ndk^2) for the main algorithm, but the analysis in Section 5 and the supplementary material must be carefully checked for hidden polylog factors. The 'win-win-win' analysis involves multiple phases (restarting, subspace removal, binary searches) that each introduce logarithmic factors. The final runtime should account for all these factors explicitly, not just the dominant term. The authors should provide a clear derivation of the runtime from the algorithm's steps, including the cost of each phase and the number of iterations. Without this, the claim of 'nearly-PCA time' may be overstated.

- Justification of the Ky Fan MMW procedure: The Ky Fan MMW procedure is central to the fast algorithm, but its convergence guarantees are only stated informally (Equation 2) and rely on several assumptions (e.g., monotonicity of gain matrices, uniform eigenvalues). The paper must provide a rigorous proof of the regret bound (2) under the approximate PCA errors from Proposition 3. The 'win-win-win' analysis introduces multiple exit criteria, and it is unclear whether the regret bound holds in all cases. The authors should present a formal theorem statement for the Ky Fan MMW procedure, including the conditions under which (2) holds and how the approximation errors from Power affect the bound.

- Necessity and relaxation of the identity-bounded covariance assumption: The paper assumes that the inlier distribution has identity-bounded covariance (Σ ⪯ I). While this is standard in the literature, it is a strong assumption that may not hold in practice unless the data is pre-whitened. The paper does not discuss how to relax this assumption or handle unknown covariance. For real-world applications, the covariance may be unknown or non-isotropic, and the algorithm's performance under such conditions is unexplored. The authors should at least mention whether their results extend to general bounded covariance (e.g., via a whitening step) or acknowledge this as a limitation.

- Tightness of output list size and error guarantee: The paper achieves an output list size of O(k) and an error guarantee of O(√(k log k)). While these are near-optimal, the paper does not explicitly discuss the gap between the upper bound and known lower bounds. For example, the lower bound on error is Ω(√k) (ignoring log factors), and the list size lower bound is Ω(k). The paper should state these gaps clearly and note whether they can be closed. Additionally, the log factor in the error may be removable with a more refined analysis, and the authors should comment on this possibility.

- Robustness to slight violations of the corruption fraction assumption: The algorithm assumes that the inlier fraction is exactly 1/k (with k known). In practice, k may be unknown or the contamination may not be exactly adversarial (e.g., the inlier fraction may be slightly larger or smaller). The paper does not discuss how robust the algorithm is to such misspecification. For example, if the true inlier fraction is 1/k' with k' ≠ k, does the algorithm still produce a useful list? The authors should either provide a robustness analysis or acknowledge this as a limitation.

- Clarity of the SIFT algorithm's termination condition: The termination condition in Algorithm 1 (Line 4) checks whether λ_k(V^T Cov_w(T) V) ≥ 4/√β. The justification for this threshold is not fully explained. The proof of Theorem 2 uses this condition to bound the error, but it is not clear why 4/√β is the right choice. The authors should provide intuition for this threshold and explain how it relates to the saturation condition. Additionally, the dependence on β (the L1 norm of weights) makes the condition dynamic, which complicates the analysis.

- Missing discussion of computational-statistical tradeoffs: The paper focuses on computational efficiency but does not discuss potential tradeoffs between runtime and statistical efficiency. For instance, the faster algorithm (FastSIFT) may have worse constants or require more samples than the slower SIFT. The paper should compare the sample complexity of the two algorithms and discuss whether the speedup comes at the cost of statistical performance. This is important for practitioners who may prioritize accuracy over speed.

- Missing concrete example demonstrating algorithm behavior: The paper does not include any worked example or simulation showing the algorithm's behavior on a specific distribution. For a theory paper claiming near-optimal runtime and error, a natural expectation is to compute the algorithm's output for a simple parametric model, such as a mixture of two Gaussians with known means and identity covariance, and verify that the list contains a candidate within the claimed error bound. Without such an example, readers cannot calibrate intuitions about how the algorithm handles the interplay between the SIFT downweighting and the Ky Fan MMW procedure. Adding a short section that instantiates the algorithm on a one-dimensional or two-dimensional mixture would make the contribution more concrete and help validate the claimed guarantees.

- No comparison to prior work on a common test case: The paper claims runtime improvements over [DKK20] and [CMY20] but never demonstrates these improvements on a shared benchmark or synthetic data setting. Even a simple numerical simulation comparing runtimes for varying n, d, and k would substantiate the claim that the new algorithm is faster in practice. The authors should design a small simulation study (e.g., with Gaussian inliers and adversarial outliers) that measures wall-clock time or iteration counts for their algorithm versus the prior methods, and report the results. This would bridge the gap between asymptotic guarantees and practical relevance.

- Underdeveloped practical implementation guidance: The paper derives theoretical quantities like the Ky Fan MMW regret bound and the SIFT scores, but provides almost no guidance on how to implement these in practice. For instance, the algorithm requires approximate k-PCA via simultaneous power iteration, but the paper does not specify how to choose the number of iterations or the error tolerance ε for real data. Similarly, the 'win-win-win' analysis involves multiple phases with restart conditions that depend on unknown parameters; a practitioner would not know how to set these thresholds. The authors should include a discussion of parameter choices, convergence criteria, and potential numerical issues (e.g., handling near-singular covariance matrices) to make the algorithm usable.

- Missing analysis of robustness to misspecified k: The algorithm assumes the inlier fraction 1/k is known exactly, but in many applications k is unknown or the contamination is not perfectly adversarial. The paper does not analyze how the algorithm behaves when the assumed k differs from the true k. For example, if the true inlier fraction is 1/(k+1) but the algorithm uses k, does the output list still contain a candidate near the true mean? The authors should either provide a robustness analysis (e.g., showing that the error degrades gracefully with misspecification) or explicitly state that the algorithm requires exact knowledge of k, which is a limitation.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide a rigorous proof of the Ky Fan MMW regret bound (Equation 2) under the approximate PCA errors, including a formal theorem statement and analysis of the 'win-win-win' cases.

- Derive the final runtime guarantee explicitly, accounting for all phases and logarithmic factors, and validate the claim of 'nearly-PCA time'.

- Discuss the necessity of the identity-bounded covariance assumption and whether the results extend to general bounded covariance or unknown covariance.

- Add a section on robustness to misspecification of the inlier fraction k, including theoretical bounds or experimental simulations.

- Clarify the termination condition in Algorithm 1 and provide intuition for the threshold 4/√β.

comments.title: Unjustified bound in Lemma 5 chain
comments.quote: \frac {\sqrt {\beta}}{\alpha} \leq \frac {k}{2}
comments.explanation: In the final inequality of Lemma 5, the bound $\frac{\sqrt{\beta}}{\alpha} \leq \frac{k}{2}$ is asserted without justification. Since $k = \lceil 4/\alpha \rceil$, we have $\frac{\sqrt{\beta}}{\alpha} \leq \frac{k\sqrt{\beta}}{4}$. To conclude $\frac{\sqrt{\beta}}{\alpha} \leq \frac{k}{2}$, one would need $\sqrt{\beta} \leq 2$, i.e., $\beta \leq 4$. However, $\beta = \|w\|_1$ can be as large as $n$ initially, and the algorithm only guarantees $\beta \geq \alpha^2$ from saturation. The bound $\frac{\sqrt{\beta}}{\alpha} \leq \frac{k}{2}$ is not generally true. This appears to be an error in the derivation. The authors should re-examine this step and provide a correct bound, or adjust the argument.

comments.title: Runtime expression inconsistency between abstract and Theorem 1
comments.quote: Our algorithm runs in time $\widetilde{O}(ndk)^2$, where $n$ is the dataset size. This runtime nearly matches the cost of performing $k$-PCA on the data, a natural bottleneck of known algorithms for (very) special cases of our problem, such as clustering well-separated mixtures.
comments.explanation: The abstract states runtime $\widetilde{O}(ndk)^2$, which is ambiguous and non-standard. It could be interpreted as $\widetilde{O}((ndk)^2)$ or $\widetilde{O}(ndk^2)$. Theorem 1 states runtime $\widetilde{O}(nd/\alpha)$, which with $k = \Theta(1/\alpha)$ becomes $\widetilde{O}(ndk)$. The abstract's expression is inconsistent with Theorem 1 and likely a typographical error. Rewrite the abstract's runtime as $\widetilde{O}(ndk)$ (or $\widetilde{O}(nd/\alpha)$) to match Theorem 1, and clarify the exponent.

comments.title: Missing justification for inequality chain in Lemma 5
comments.quote: \begin{array}{l} \sum_ {i \in S} \frac {w _ {i}}{\| w _ {S} \| _ {1}} \tau_ {i} = \sum_ {i \in S} \frac {w _ {i}}{\| w _ {S} \| _ {1}} \left\| \boldsymbol {\Sigma} ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \mathbf {V} ^ {\top} \left(X _ {i} - \mu_ {w} (T)\right) \right\| _ {2} ^ {2} \\ = \sum_ {i \in S} \frac {w _ {i}}{\| w _ {S} \| _ {1}} \left(\left\| \boldsymbol {\Sigma} ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \mathbf {V} ^ {\top} \left(X _ {i} - \mu_ {w} (S)\right) \right\| _ {2} ^ {2} + \left\| \boldsymbol {\Sigma} ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \mathbf {V} ^ {\top} \left(\mu_ {w} (S) - \mu_ {w} (T)\right) \right\| _ {2} ^ {2}\right) \\ = \left\langle \boldsymbol {\Sigma} ^ {- 1}, \mathbf {V} ^ {\top} \operatorname {C o v} _ {w} (S) \mathbf {V} \right\rangle + \left\| \boldsymbol {\Sigma} ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \mathbf {V} ^ {\top} \left(\mu_ {w} (S) - \mu_ {w} (T)\right) \right\| _ {2} ^ {2} \\ \leq \left\langle \boldsymbol {\Sigma} ^ {- 1}, \frac {\alpha}{\| w _ {S} \| _ {1}} \mathbf {I} \right\rangle + \frac {\| w \| _ {1}}{\| w _ {S} \| _ {1}} \leq \frac {1}{4} \left\langle \sqrt {\beta} \mathbf {I}, \frac {1}{\sqrt {\beta}} \mathbf {I} \right\rangle + \frac {\sqrt {\beta}}{\alpha} \leq \frac {k}{2}. \\ \end{array}
comments.explanation: The inequality chain in Lemma 5 skips several steps. The bound $\langle \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}, \frac{\alpha}{\|w_S\|_1} \mathbf{I} \rangle \leq \frac{1}{4} \langle \sqrt{\beta} \mathbf{I}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{\beta}} \mathbf{I} \rangle$ requires two implicit facts: (i) $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \preceq \frac{\sqrt{\beta}}{4} \mathbf{I}$, which follows from the exit condition $\lambda_k(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}) \geq 4/\sqrt{\beta}$ (since $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ is $k\times k$, $\lambda_{\min}(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}) = \lambda_k(\boldsymbol{\Sigma})$), and (ii) $\frac{\alpha}{\|w_S\|_1} \leq \frac{1}{\sqrt{\beta}}$, which follows from Definition 2 ($\|w_S\|_1 \geq \alpha \sqrt{\beta}$). The step from $\frac{\|w\|_1}{\|w_S\|_1}$ to $\frac{\sqrt{\beta}}{\alpha}$ also uses $\|w_S\|_1 \geq \alpha \sqrt{\beta}$ and $\|w\|_1 = \beta$. The final bound $k/2$ is then $\frac{k}{4} + \frac{\sqrt{\beta}}{\alpha}$, but the paper writes $\frac{k}{2}$ without showing that $\frac{\sqrt{\beta}}{\alpha} \leq \frac{k}{4}$. Since $k = \lceil 4/\alpha \rceil$, we have $\frac{\sqrt{\beta}}{\alpha} \leq \frac{\sqrt{\beta}}{4/k} = \frac{k\sqrt{\beta}}{4}$, but $\beta$ is not bounded above. The derivation of $k/2$ is incomplete. Rewrite the inequality chain with explicit justifications for each step, including the use of saturation and the exit condition, and verify the final bound.

comments.title: Unclear derivation of operator norm bound in Theorem 2
comments.quote:
To bound this second term, we apply Lemma 2 on the set of points $\{(\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{P})X_{i}\}_{i\in T}$. This implies

$\|(\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{P})\left(\mu_{w}(T)-\mu^{\star}\right)\|_{2}^{2}\leq\frac{2\beta}{\|w_{S}\|_{1}}\left\|(\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{P})\text{Cov}_{w}(T)(\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{P})\right\|_{\text{op}}+\frac{2\alpha}{\beta}\leq\frac{12\sqrt{\beta}}{\|w_{S}\|_{1}}+\frac{2\alpha}{\beta}\leq\frac{14}{\alpha}.$
comments.explanation: The bound $\|(\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{P})\text{Cov}_w(T)(\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{P})\|_{\text{op}} \leq 1.2 \lambda_k(\text{Cov}_w(T))$ is not directly justified. Proposition 2 guarantees $\|(\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{V}\mathbf{V}^\top) \mathbf{A} (\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{V}\mathbf{V}^\top)\|_{\text{op}} \leq (1+\epsilon)\lambda_{k+1}(\mathbf{A})$, not $\lambda_k(\mathbf{A})$. The paper uses $\lambda_k$ instead of $\lambda_{k+1}$, which is a minor error since $\lambda_{k+1} \leq \lambda_k$, but the justification should reference $\lambda_{k+1}$. The subsequent inequality $1.2 \lambda_k(\text{Cov}_w(T)) \leq 1.5 \lambda_k(\mathbf{\Sigma})$ also needs justification from the Power approximation guarantee (Proposition 2 gives $\langle \mathbf{V}_{:i}, \mathbf{A} \mathbf{V}_{:i} \rangle \in [1\pm\epsilon]\lambda_i(\mathbf{A})$, so $\lambda_k(\mathbf{\Sigma}) \approx \lambda_k(\mathbf{A})$). The paper should clarify these steps and correct the eigenvalue index.

comments.title: Optimal sample complexity claim unsupported
comments.quote: Our main result is a new algorithm for bounded covariance distributions with optimal sample complexity and near-optimal error guarantee, running in nearly-PCA time.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims 'optimal sample complexity' but does not specify what optimal means or cite a matching lower bound. The paper later states the sample complexity is $n = \Omega(d/\alpha)$ (from Proposition 1 and Assumption 1), but it is not shown that this is information-theoretically optimal for list-decodable mean estimation under identity-bounded covariance. The claim of optimality is unsupported in the abstract and the main text. Either provide a citation to a known lower bound (e.g., [DKK20] or [CMY20]) or qualify the claim as 'near-optimal' or 'matching known lower bounds up to logarithmic factors'.

comments.title: Proposition 1 reference is ambiguous
comments.quote:
Proposition 1 (Proposition B.1, *[x10]*).

For any constant $\epsilon\in(0,1)$, there are constants $c,C>0$ such that with probability at least $1-\exp(-\Omega(n))$, for $n=\frac{Cd}{\alpha}$, if an $(1+\epsilon)\alpha$ fraction of points in $\{X_{i}\}_{i\in T}\subseteq\mathbb{R}^{d}$ is drawn from $\mathcal{D}$ with covariance bounded by $c\mathbf{I}$, then Assumption 1 holds.
comments.explanation: The placeholder '*[x10]*' is not a proper citation. The proposition should be rewritten to explicitly state that the subset $S$ exists with the required property, and the placeholder should be replaced with a proper citation (e.g., 'Proposition B.1 of [DKK20]'). Rewrite the proposition as: 'For any constant $\epsilon\in(0,1)$, there exist constants $c,C>0$ such that with probability at least $1-\exp(-\Omega(n))$, for $n = C d/\alpha$, if an $(1+\epsilon)\alpha$ fraction of points in $\{X_i\}_{i\in T}$ is drawn from $\mathcal{D}$ with $\operatorname{Cov}(\mathcal{D}) \preceq c\mathbf{I}$, then there exists a subset $S\subseteq T$ of size $\alpha n$ satisfying $\mathbb{E}_{i\sim S}[(X_i-\mu^*)(X_i-\mu^*)^\top] \preceq \mathbf{I}$.'

comments.title: Assumption 1 uses undefined expectation notation
comments.quote:
Assumption 1.

$\exists S\subseteq\{X_{i}\}_{i\in T}\subseteq\mathbb{R}^{d}$ of size $\alpha n=\Theta(d)$ with $\mathbb{E}_{i\sim S}[(X_{i}-\mu^{*})\,(X_{i}-\mu^{*})^{\top}]\preceq\mathbf{I}$.
comments.explanation: The notation $\mathbb{E}_{i\sim S}$ is defined earlier in the 'Distributions' paragraph as 'uniform samples $i$ from a set $S$', but this definition is not explicitly referenced in Assumption 1. For clarity, rewrite the assumption using explicit summation: '$\exists S\subseteq\{X_i\}_{i\in T}\subseteq\mathbb{R}^d$ of size $\alpha n = \Theta(d)$ such that $\frac{1}{|S|}\sum_{i\in S}(X_i-\mu^*)(X_i-\mu^*)^\top \preceq \mathbf{I}$.'

comments.title: Definition of probability simplex is non-standard
comments.quote: denote the (solid) probability simplex in  $n$  dimensions by  $\Delta^n = \{w \in \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}^n \mid \| w \|_1 \leq 1\}$ .
comments.explanation: The definition of $\Delta^n$ as $\{w \in \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}^n \mid \|w\|_1 \leq 1\}$ is non-standard: the probability simplex is typically defined as $\{w \in \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}^n \mid \|w\|_1 = 1\}$. The paper's definition includes sub-probability vectors (sum less than 1), which is unusual and may cause confusion later when weights are normalized. The paper should clarify why the sum is allowed to be less than 1, or change the definition to the standard one. If the intention is to allow weights that sum to less than 1 (e.g., for subsets), this should be explicitly stated and justified.

comments.title: Eigenvalue uniformity condition not justified
comments.quote:
bf{G}_{t}\preceq\mathbf{I}$. If we can guarantee that every $\left\langle\mathbf{G}_{t},\mathbf{Y}_{t}\right\rangle$ is bounded by, say, $\frac{1}{5}\left\|\mathbf{G}_{0}\right\|_{k}$, and $k\left\|\mathbf{G}_{0}\right\|_{\text{op}}\leq 2\left\|\mathbf{G}_{0}\right\|_{k}$ (i.e. the top $k$ eigenvalues of $\mathbf{G}_{0}$ are roughly uniform), the above regret guarantee becomes

$\left\|\mathbf{G}_{T}\right\|_{k}\leq\left\|\frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=0}^{T-1}\mathbf{G}_{t}\right\|_{k}\leq\frac{2}{5}\left\|\mathbf{G}_{0}\right\|_{k}+\frac{2\left\|\mathbf{G}_{0}\right\|_{k}\log d}{T}\,.$

Now, $T=O(\log d)$ iterat
comments.explanation: The derivation of the displayed inequality from the regret guarantee (2) relies on the condition $k\|\mathbf{G}_0\|_{\text{op}} \le 2\|\mathbf{G}_0\|_k$, which requires the top $k$ eigenvalues of $\mathbf{G}_0$ to be roughly uniform. The paper does not establish that this condition holds for the initial empirical covariance matrix under the adversarial contamination model. The text later acknowledges this as an open question (item 2) but then proceeds to use the inequality as if the condition can be enforced. The authors should either prove that the condition can be guaranteed (e.g., via the restarting strategy described) or derive a modified bound that does not require uniformity. Rewrite the quoted passage to include a justification or a reference to a lemma that establishes the uniformity condition.

comments.title: Monotonicity of gain matrices not established
comments.quote: How do we define the sequence $\{\mathbf{G}_{t}\}_{t\geq 0}$ so that it is monotonically decreasing? For instance, our safety condition (Definition 3) is defined with respect to normalized scores, but normalizing the covariance matrices makes them no longer (necessarily) monotone.
comments.explanation: The text correctly identifies that the gain matrices $\mathbf{G}_t$ are not guaranteed to be monotonically non-increasing under the safe weight removal rule (1). The regret guarantee (2) used earlier requires monotonicity to set $\eta = \|\mathbf{G}_0\|_{\text{op}}^{-1}$ and ensure $\eta \mathbf{G}_t \preceq \mathbf{I}$ for all $t$. The paper then proposes a restarting strategy to handle this, but the description is vague: it says "We restart the algorithm in phases where the $\ell_1$ norm of the weights halves, so that in each phase the normalizing constant is stable." It is not explained how halving the $\ell_1$ norm of weights ensures monotonicity of the gain matrices within a phase. Without a rigorous argument, the claim that the regret bound applies within each phase is unsupported. The authors should provide a formal lemma showing that under the restarting schedule, the sequence $\{\mathbf{G}_t\}$ within a phase is monotonically non-increasing, or else modify the algorithm to enforce this property.

comments.title: Binary search guarantee for inner product bound unclear
comments.quote: Whenever $\Theta(\log d)$ iterations pass without meeting either of the above “exit criteria,” we use binary searches to safely remove as much weight as possible so each covariance matrix $\mathbf{G}_{t}$ meets the inner product criteria through $\mathbf{Y}_{t}$ to progress, i.e. we use weight removal to bound the right hand side of the regret guarantee (2).
comments.explanation: The text claims that binary searches can be used to remove weight so that $\langle \mathbf{G}_t, \mathbf{Y}_t \rangle \le \frac15 \|\mathbf{G}_0\|_k$ holds. However, the relationship between weight removal and the inner product $\langle \mathbf{G}_t, \mathbf{Y}_t \rangle$ is not derived. The matrix $\mathbf{Y}_t$ is the MMW iterate, which depends on the entire history of gain matrices. It is not obvious that removing weight (i.e., downweighting certain points) can directly control this inner product without breaking the safety condition or the monotonicity of the gain matrices. The paper does not provide a lemma or reference showing that such binary searches exist and terminate in polylogarithmic time. This step is critical to the polylogarithmic iteration count, but the argument is only sketched. The authors should either provide a rigorous proof or cite a specific result that establishes this claim.

comments.title: Restarting strategy may increase dimension beyond claimed bound
comments.quote: We restart the algorithm whenever the $k^{\text{th}}$ largest eigenvalue of the covariance matrix is smaller than half the largest, setting aside the $k$ eigendirections. The remainder of the algorithm works in the space orthogonal to these directions. Since each time we set aside $k$ directions we halve the operator norm on the remaining subspace, this only occurs logarithmically many times, and yields a “set-aside subspace” of dimension $\widetilde{O}(k)$.
comments.explanation: The claim that the set-aside subspace has dimension $\widetilde{O}(k)$ assumes that each restart sets aside exactly $k$ directions and that the number of restarts is $O(\log d)$. However, the condition for restarting is that the $k^{\text{th}}$ eigenvalue is smaller than half the largest eigenvalue. This condition could be triggered even when the top $k$ eigenvalues are not all large, potentially requiring more than $k$ directions to be set aside per restart to achieve the halving of operator norm. The paper does not argue that setting aside exactly $k$ directions per restart suffices to halve the operator norm on the remaining subspace. Without a rigorous bound on the number of directions set aside per restart, the total dimension of the set-aside subspace could exceed $\widetilde{O}(k)$. Add a lemma showing that under the restart condition, the top $k$ eigenvectors span a subspace whose removal reduces the operator norm by at least a factor of 2.

comments.title: Proposition 3 approximation factor mismatch
comments.quote:
ary material.

Proposition 3 (Informal, see Proposition 7 of the supplementary material). Let  $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{V}\mathbf{V}^{\top}$  where  $\mathbf{V} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$  is the result of  $\widetilde{O}(\epsilon^{-1})$  iterations of simultaneous power iteration (approximate top- $k$  eigenvectors) on  $\mathbf{S} \in \mathbb{S}_{\geq 0}^{d}$ . Then with high probability,

$$
(1 - \epsilon) \mathbf {S} \preceq \mathbf {P S P} + (\mathbf {I} - \mathbf {P}) \mathbf {S} (\mathbf {I} - \mathbf {P}) \preceq (1 + \epsilon) \mathbf {S}.
$$

This improves a similar analysis in [CMY20], which showed an approximation factor of  $1 \pm k\epsilon$
comments.explanation: For a general projector $\mathbf{P}$ that does not commute with $\mathbf{S}$, the expression $\mathbf{P S P} + (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{P}) \mathbf{S} (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{P})$ omits the cross terms $\mathbf{P S (I-P)}$ and $(\mathbf{I-P}) \mathbf{S P}$, so it is not equal to $\mathbf{S}$. However, the claim in Proposition 3 is about an inequality, not an equality. The inequality $\mathbf{A} \preceq \mathbf{B}$ means $\mathbf{B} - \mathbf{A}$ is PSD. For the inequality to hold, the cross terms must be accounted for. Standard guarantees for approximate PCA (e.g., from simultaneous power iteration) bound $\|\mathbf{S} - \mathbf{V} \mathbf{V}^\top \mathbf{S} \mathbf{V} \mathbf{V}^\top\|_{\text{op}}$ or similar, but they do not directly give the claimed two-sided inequality without additional structure (e.g., $\mathbf{S}$ being nearly low-rank). The authors claim this improves over $1 \pm k\epsilon$ from [CMY20], but the cited work likely had a different expression. Without seeing the formal Proposition 7, the claim as stated is not obviously derivable from standard approximate PCA guarantees. The authors should either provide a rigorous derivation or clarify the exact approximation guarantee. Rewrite the informal statement to reflect the actual bound (e.g., a spectral norm bound on the difference) or add a reference to the formal proposition that justifies the inequality.

comments.title: Error guarantee constant not specified
comments.quote: Assuming the ground truth distribution on $\mathbb{R}^d$ has identity-bounded covariance, our algorithm outputs $O(k)$ candidate means, one of which is within distance $O(\sqrt{k \log k})$ from the truth.
comments.explanation: The error guarantee $O(\sqrt{k \log k})$ is stated without a constant or a reference to where it is derived. The paper's Theorem 2 (in Section 4) gives an error bound of $\sqrt{22/\alpha} = O(\sqrt{k})$ for the warmup SIFT algorithm, but the main algorithm's error is claimed to be $O(\sqrt{k \log k})$. The abstract does not explain why the log factor appears or whether it is tight. The paper should either state the exact bound (e.g., $C\sqrt{k \log k}$ for some explicit $C$) or cite the theorem in the supplementary material that proves this bound. Without this, the claim is vague and unverifiable from the abstract alone.

comments.title: Incomplete justification due to page break in Lemma 5
comments.quote: The second inequality used Definition 2 twice, which implies that  $\| w_S\| _1\geq \alpha \sqrt{\beta}$ , as well as the exit condition in
comments.explanation: The sentence is cut off by a page break, making the reference to the exit condition incomplete. The full condition is from Algorithm 1 Line 4: $\lambda_k(\mathbf{V}^\top \text{Cov}_{w^{(t)}}(T) \mathbf{V}) \geq 4/\sqrt{\beta}$. The proof should explicitly state this condition and show how it implies $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \preceq \frac{\sqrt{\beta}}{4} \mathbf{I}$. Additionally, the step $\frac{\alpha}{\|w_S\|_1} \leq \frac{1}{\sqrt{\beta}}$ from Definition 2 should be explicitly stated. Complete the sentence and add the missing justification.
